source_paper_title: Counting and Enumerating Optimum Cut Sets for Hypergraph k-Partitioning Problems for Fixed k
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Beideman_2024, title={Counting and Enumerating Optimum Cut Sets for Hypergraph k-Partitioning Problems for Fixed k}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0259}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0259}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Beideman, Calvin and Chandrasekaran, Karthekeyan and Wang, Weihang}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2579–2601} }
context:
Page 24, Section 7 (Conclusion)

Resolving the complexity of this variant of the hypergraph k-partitioning problem for \(k \ge 5\) remains open.
title: Determine complexity of minimizing sum of boundary cut sizes in hypergraph k-partitions
background_latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a (multi-)hypergraph with vertex set \(V\) of size \(n\) and hyperedge set \(E\). Each hyperedge \(e\in E\) is a subset of \(V\). (The paper primarily discusses the unweighted case, i.e., each hyperedge has unit cost; the objective below is stated for this setting.)

For a nonempty proper subset \(U\subset V\), define the cut-set
\[\delta(U):=\{e\in E: e\cap U\neq\emptyset \text{ and } e\cap (V\setminus U)\neq\emptyset\},\]
and its cut value \(d(U):=|\delta(U)|\).

A \(k\)-partition of \(V\) is an ordered tuple \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\) of pairwise disjoint nonempty subsets whose union is \(V\).

For a \(k\)-partition \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\), the paper considers various objectives based on the boundary sizes \(|\delta(V_i)|\). In particular, the variant highlighted in the conclusion asks to minimize the sum of boundary cut values
\[\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| = \sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i).\]
problem_definition_latex:
Given a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\) and a fixed integer \(k\ge 5\), determine the computational complexity of the optimization problem
\[\min\left\{\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| : (V_1,\dots,V_k) \text{ is a } k\text{-partition of } V\right\}.\]
In particular, decide whether this problem admits a polynomial-time algorithm for fixed \(k\ge 5\) (or whether it is NP-hard) in the unweighted hypergraph setting.
latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a (multi-)hypergraph with vertex set \(V\) of size \(n\) and hyperedge set \(E\). Each hyperedge \(e\in E\) is a subset of \(V\). (The paper primarily discusses the unweighted case, i.e., each hyperedge has unit cost; the objective below is stated for this setting.)

For a nonempty proper subset \(U\subset V\), define the cut-set
\[\delta(U):=\{e\in E: e\cap U\neq\emptyset \text{ and } e\cap (V\setminus U)\neq\emptyset\},\]
and its cut value \(d(U):=|\delta(U)|\).

A \(k\)-partition of \(V\) is an ordered tuple \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\) of pairwise disjoint nonempty subsets whose union is \(V\).

For a \(k\)-partition \((V_1,\dots,V_k)\), the paper considers various objectives based on the boundary sizes \(|\delta(V_i)|\). In particular, the variant highlighted in the conclusion asks to minimize the sum of boundary cut values
\[\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| = \sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i).\]

Given a hypergraph \(G=(V,E)\) and a fixed integer \(k\ge 5\), determine the computational complexity of the optimization problem
\[\min\left\{\sum_{i=1}^k |\delta(V_i)| : (V_1,\dots,V_k) \text{ is a } k\text{-partition of } V\right\}.\]
In particular, decide whether this problem admits a polynomial-time algorithm for fixed \(k\ge 5\) (or whether it is NP-hard) in the unweighted hypergraph setting.
open_problem_quote: Resolving the complexity of this variant of the hypergraph k-partitioning problem for \(k \ge 5\) remains open.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is a complexity-status question (polynomial-time solvable vs NP-hard) rather than a universal inequality that can be refuted by a finite instance search. While one can experiment with heuristics or reductions, automated counterexample search is not a faithful route to resolving the stated problem.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary coding loop could explore algorithmic ideas (e.g., dynamic programming for fixed \(k\), relaxations, or exact solvers on structured instances) and test performance, but empirical success would not establish polynomial-time solvability or hardness. It may still help by discovering patterns or candidate reductions/algorithms that can later be proved.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The objective in the open problem is to minimize \(\sum_{i=1}^k d(V_i)\), where \(d(U)=|\delta(U)|\) is the hypergraph cut function. The source paper (Beideman–Chandrasekaran–Wang) emphasizes that this objective is distinct from Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut (minimizing \(|\delta(V_1,\dots,V_k)|\)) and from Minmax-Hypergraph-\(k\)-Partition (minimizing \(\max_i d(V_i)\)). Their main structural tool (recovery of optimum cut-sets via small terminal sets) successfully yields deterministic polynomial-time algorithms for fixed \(k\) for Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut and for Minmax-Hypergraph-\(k\)-Partition, and even enumeration of all optimum cut-sets for these objectives. However, they explicitly note that “natural variants” of their structural theorem fail for the min-sum-of-boundaries objective, leaving its complexity unresolved for fixed \(k\ge 5\).

Related work provides partial context but not a resolution. Lawler’s early paper introduced hypergraph cutset/partition formulations, while later divide-and-conquer frameworks (Okumoto–Fukunaga–Nagamochi) treat several hypergraph/submodular partitioning objectives and highlight the broader connection to submodular \(k\)-partitioning. On the algorithmic side, Chandrasekaran–Chekuri established polynomial-time solvability for fixed \(k\) of Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut and of minmax partitioning for symmetric submodular functions (hence Minmax-Hypergraph-\(k\)-Partition), and subsequent work enumerated all minimum \(k\)-cut-sets. These results suggest that terminal-cut structure can tame certain hypergraph partitioning objectives for fixed \(k\), but they do not extend to minimizing \(\sum_i d(V_i)\), which remains a key gap.

Promising directions suggested implicitly by the source paper are: (i) identify a new structural characterization tailored to \(\sum_i d(V_i)\) (since direct analogues of Theorem 1.4 fail), perhaps via alternative uncrossing/laminarity phenomena for collections \(\{V_i\}\); (ii) attempt hardness reductions for fixed \(k\ge 5\) exploiting the fact that \(\sum_i d(V_i)\) counts each hyperedge proportionally to the number of parts it touches (unlike Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut which counts each crossing hyperedge once), potentially enabling encoding of constraint satisfaction; and (iii) study restricted hypergraph classes (bounded rank, bounded degree, or linear hypergraphs) where the objective might become tractable and could guide the general case.

sources.title: Cutsets and Partitions of Hypergraphs
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces early formulations of hypergraph cutset/partition problems, including objectives based on hyperedge boundary/cutsets.
sources.connection: Provides historical context for hypergraph partitioning objectives; does not resolve complexity of minimizing \(\sum_i |\delta(V_i)|\).

sources.title: Divide-and-conquer algorithms for partitioning hypergraphs and submodular systems
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops divide-and-conquer frameworks for several hypergraph/submodular partitioning objectives, highlighting tractable cases and structural approaches.
sources.connection: Related algorithmic techniques for hypergraph/submodular partitioning; does not settle the specific min-sum-of-boundaries objective for fixed \(k\ge 5\).

sources.title: Hypergraph k-cut for fixed k in deterministic polynomial time
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut for fixed \(k\), using structural properties of hypergraph cut functions and terminal cuts.
sources.connection: Shows some hypergraph \(k\)-partitioning objectives are polytime for fixed \(k\), but explicitly distinguishes Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut from minimizing \(\sum_i |\delta(V_i)|\).

sources.title: Min-max partitioning of hypergraphs and symmetric submodular functions
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for Minmax-SymSubmod-\(k\)-Partition for fixed \(k\), implying polytime solvability of Minmax-Hypergraph-\(k\)-Partition.
sources.connection: Contrasts with the open min-sum-of-boundaries objective; indicates minmax variants are tractable while minsum variants remain open for larger \(k\).

sources.title: Deterministic enumeration of all minimum k-cut-sets in hypergraphs for fixed k
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives deterministic enumeration of all minimum \(k\)-cut-sets for Hypergraph-\(k\)-Cut for fixed \(k\), via terminal-cut structure and uncrossing.
sources.connection: Develops structural/algorithmic machinery for hypergraph cut objectives; does not address minimizing \(\sum_i |\delta(V_i)|\).

sources.title: Counting and enumerating optimum cut sets for hypergraph k-partitioning problems for fixed k
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves structural theorems (notably Theorem 1.4) enabling enumeration of min-\(k\)-cut-sets and minmax-\(k\)-cut-sets in hypergraphs for fixed \(k\); explicitly states the min-sum-of-boundaries objective \(\sum_i |\delta(V_i)|\) remains open for \(k\ge 5\).
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open problem; it clarifies that their terminal-cut techniques do not extend to the \(\sum_i |\delta(V_i)|\) objective and leaves complexity open for fixed \(k\ge 5\).
